title: From Incubator to Approval: How Mentorship Helped the Runyankore Wikipedia Community Grow
content:
What does it take to build a Wikipedia in a language whose contributors often edit from mobile phones, while working through an interface that many find difficult to navigate? For the Runyankore Wikipedia community, the answer has been persistence, practical mentorship, stronger leadership, and a growing network of people and institutions willing to support the work.

At the time of publication, the Language Committee has approved the creation of Runyankore Wikipedia. The project is now awaiting the remaining technical work needed to create its independent site. That approval is a major achievement, but it is not the whole story. Behind it are volunteers who continued contributing despite technical and financial barriers, a community leader learning how to share responsibility, a mentor who helped turn broad ambitions into practical steps, and university students discovering that they could contribute to knowledge in their own language.

This is the story of what happened when the Runyankore community and the Wikimedia Language Diversity Hub worked together.
snippet: What does it take to build a Wikipedia in a language whose contributors often edit from mobile phones, while working through an interface that many find difficult to navigate? For the Runyankore
creator: Owobusingye

title: Data, Dignity, and Difference: Centering Feminist Voices on Wikimedia Projects
content:
When knowledge about women is missing, incomplete, or difficult to find, we not just lose more than information; we lose histories, perspectives, and the opportunity to build a more representative and inclusive record of our world.

Across Wikimedia projects, significant gender gaps continue to shape what knowledge is available online. Although countless women have influenced politics, education, science, social justice, literature, and community development, many of their stories remain absent or underdeveloped across Wikipedia and Wikidata. This challenge is particularly evidenced through the African and non-African feminist leaders, organizations, movements, and initiatives whose contributions are often overlooked or insufficiently documented. Addressing these gaps requires more than creating new articles; it involves ensuring that the existing one are equitably represented across other languages; it calls for building communities that understand the importance of equitable knowledge production and are equipped with the skills to contribute meaningfully.
snippet: When knowledge about women is missing, incomplete, or difficult to find, we not just lose more than information; we lose histories, perspectives, and the opportunity to build a more representative and
creator: Iwuala Lucy

title: Mentor Me! 2026 (First Stream)
content:
The Mentor Me! 2026 program returned this year to support both new and existing Wikimedians in building practical skills across Wikimedia projects like Wikipedia, Wikidata, and Wikimedia Commons. Organized by the Tyap Wikimedians User Group, the program brought together over 70 registered participants for seven weeks of learning, mentorship, and collaboration. The Stream 1 sessions were held online on Fridays via Jitsi Meet and in-person on Saturdays at AX1, Aliyu Makama Road, Barnawa, Kaduna, Nigeria, making it possible for participants to learn regardless of their location.

Through hands-on training led by experienced Wikimedia contributors from within and outside Nigeria, and former mentees who participated in the 2025 edition, participants explored topics ranging from citation and referencing, Wikidata editing, and language documentation to templates, the OpenRefine tool, and other Wikimedia tools, while gaining the confidence to make meaningful contributions to the Wikimedia Movement.
snippet: The Mentor Me! 2026 program returned this year to support both new and existing Wikimedians in building practical skills across Wikimedia projects like Wikipedia, Wikidata, and Wikimedia Commons.
creator: Zasan Doshiyak

title: Reflections from Around the Puzzle Globe
content:
This blog is being translated on Meta-Wiki.

Dear Wikimedians,

I joined the Wikimedia Foundation six months ago, at the beginning of Wikipedia’s 25th birthday. It’s been an extraordinary introduction to this movement.

I have met with more than 1,100 Wikimedians through one-on-one conversations and messages with volunteers and affiliates; Around the Puzzle Globe calls; community events like Futures Lab, WikiCredCon, and the ESEAP conference; and visits to communities in Tokyo, Lisbon, Cape Town, and Johannesburg. Valuable perspectives came from contributors of all kinds—those who are well known among other Wikimedians, those who quietly build Wikimedia projects every day, and even everyday readers of Wikipedia.

What struck me most is the depth of this movement’s commitment to free knowledge. Whether writing and editing articles, mentoring newcomers, building tools, organizing events, translating content, creating partnerships, or serving through affiliates, Wikimedians contribute in countless ways, and every contribution matters.

Across these conversations, one theme emerged consistently: we must adapt to remain relevant in this period of profound change for the internet and Wikimedia.
snippet:
This blog is being translated on Meta-Wiki.

Dear Wikimedians,
creator: Eureka Endo

title: Documenting the Heritage of Indigenous Peoples: A Training Session by Muj(lh)eres Latinoamericanas en Wikimedia
content:
Imagine a world in which every single human being can freely share in the sum of all knowledge. That’s our commitment.

One of the greatest strengths of the Wikimedia movement is that anyone can contribute to making knowledge about their community, culture, and history accessible to the entire world. This was the goal of a training workshop organized by the Escuela de lideresas (School of Women Leaders), a program of Muj(lh)eres Latinoamericanas en Wikimedia (MLA), in which women participants learned how to edit Wikipedia and Wikimedia Commons while documenting a cultural event of great significance to Bolivia.
snippet: Imagine a world in which every single human being can freely share in the sum of all knowledge. That’s our commitment.
creator: Jaluj

title: How to strengthen the public domain with Wikidata
content:
Since 2025, a group of initiatives from Latin America that share a common vision on how to strengthen the cultural heritage of our territories have come together to launch the regional project “Strengthening the Latin American public domain with Wikidata”. In this post we tell you why Wikidata is an excellent tool for improving information on heritage in countries of the Global South, what strategies we consider successful, and how to replicate the initiative in other places.

Access to cultural heritage works in Latin America faces severe limitations due to the scarcity, dispersion, and lack of systematization of data on authors and their copyright status. The lack of open and accessible data hinders digitization projects and disproportionately affects the visibility of marginalized cultural expressions (those of women, diverse groups, and racialized communities).

Added to this are the lengthy copyright terms and the legislative complexities that vary from country to country. Not knowing the legal status of works generates fear of infringing rights, which inhibits the free use of materials that are already in the public domain.
snippet: Since 2025, a group of initiatives from Latin America that share a common vision on how to strengthen the cultural heritage of our territories have come together to launch the regional project
creator: Jorge Gemetto

title: When Football Meets Free Knowledge: How Wikimedia Usergroup Nigeria Celebrated World Football Day Through Wikipedia
content:
Every weekend, millions of Nigerians gather in stadiums, viewing centers, homes, and street corners to watch football. They celebrate unforgettable goals, debate legendary players, relive historic victories, and proudly support their favorite clubs.

Football is woven into everyday life; it sparks conversations, builds friendships, unites communities, and inspires generations. Yet many of Nigeria’s football stories remain undocumented, as countless players, clubs, competitions, coaches, and defining moments that have shaped the nation’s sporting history are still missing from the world’s largest free encyclopedia.

If these stories are not documented today, future generations may never know them.

That question inspired the Wikimedia User Group Nigeria to celebrate the World Football Day campaign 2026 differently. Rather than simply commemorating an international observance, we asked ourselves one simple question:

How can football become a gateway to free knowledge?

How can football become a gateway to free knowledge?
snippet: Every weekend, millions of Nigerians gather in stadiums, viewing centers, homes, and street corners to watch football. They celebrate unforgettable goals, debate legendary players, relive historic
creator: Uncle Bash007

title: Applications Now Open for Wiki Leadership X: Building the Next Generation of Wikimedia Leaders
content:
Across the Wikimedia movement, many of our strongest community leaders never planned to become leaders.

Some became organizers because they were the most active editor in their community. Others stepped forward because someone needed to coordinate a campaign, manage a grant, or represent an affiliate. Many simply answered the call when leadership vacancies emerged.

While these journeys have produced remarkable leaders, they have also revealed a challenge shared by many Wikimedia communities around the world.

Being a great Wikimedian does not automatically prepare someone to lead a Wikimedia community.

Editing articles, organizing campaigns, facilitating trainings, or contributing thousands of edits are incredible achievements. However, leading volunteers, managing people, resolving conflicts, building partnerships, making strategic decisions, designing sustainable programs, governing affiliates, and inspiring communities require an entirely different set of competencies.

These are leadership skills that many volunteers have had little opportunity to intentionally develop.
snippet: Across the Wikimedia movement, many of our strongest community leaders never planned to become leaders.
creator: Ayokanmi WUGN

title: Draft Grantmaking Strategy launched for consultation by the GRDC
content:
The Global Resource Distribution Committee has published a proposal for a refreshed Grantmaking Strategy for the Wikimedia movement. This strategy is the result of a year-long process. It draws on consultation with a wide range of communities, affiliates, Regional Fund Committees, the Ecosystem of Wikimedia Organizations pilot, AffCom recommendations, and several Wikimedia Foundation teams.

The overarching goal is to create a grantmaking system that enables a more equitable, sustainable and impactful Wikimedia Movement. This strategy aims to:

  • Improve fairness, transparency and consistency in funding decisions.
  • Remove barriers to funding for emerging and underrepresented communities.
  • Strengthen the long-term sustainability of Wikimedia affiliates.
  • Ensure funding is more clearly linked to strategic priorities and measurable impact.
  • Empower regional decision-making while maintaining shared movement priorities.

    • Ultimately, the strategy seeks to strengthen Wikimedia communities worldwide, support contributors and readers, increase access to reliable free knowledge, and build a more resilient movement.
snippet: The Global Resource Distribution Committee has published a proposal for a refreshed Grantmaking Strategy for the Wikimedia movement. This strategy is the result of a year-long process. It draws on
creator: Qgil-WMF

title: My Afrodemics Journey: From Wikipedia Editor to Article Creator
content:
My Afrodemics journey began with a desire to grow as a Wikimedia contributor and strengthen my skills in documenting knowledge on open platforms. Before joining the program, I was already editing existing published articles, but I had not yet created and published any article on English Wikipedia. I knew that English Wikipedia was a strict platform with high standards, so I wanted to learn how to contribute more confidently and correctly.

Through the three-month Afrodemics program, I was introduced to new ways of contributing to Wikimedia projects, especially through Wikidata and English Wikipedia. The program gave me an opportunity to learn, practice, ask questions, and improve step by step. Below are the items I created and contributed to:

Afua Ntoaduro (Q138654053)

Sylvia Gyan (Q138678626)

Sarah E. Kisanga (Q138654692)

Magen Mutepfa(Q138705388)

During the program, I learned how to create Wikidata items, particularly for academics and lecturers. I also learned how to add important identifiers and external profiles such as Google Scholar and ResearchGate. This helped me understand how Wikidata connects information and makes academic profiles more visible and accessible.
snippet: My Afrodemics journey began with a desire to grow as a Wikimedia contributor and strengthen my skills in documenting knowledge on open platforms. Before joining the program, I was already editing
creator: VNGao

title: Obinna Tony: Making Queer Histories Visible in Nigeria
content: Last month, we had the opportunity to speak with Obinna Tony-Francis Ochem (Lagos, Nigeria, 1996, pronouns: he/they). Obinna, who lives in Lagos, is a Nigerian writer, human rights researcher, reporter, journalist, and LGBTQIA+  activist with a degree in Marine Sciences. He is currently the community manager for Wiki for Human Rights, Nigeria, a committee member of the Nigeria National Funding Program (Rapid Fund), and a core organizer for Queering Wiki 2026. Obinna first became involved in theWikimedia universe after attending a “Wiki Loves Pride” edit-a-thon in Lagos, in July 2025. While his involvement with the Wikimedia movement is relatively recent, Obinna more than makes up for it with his dedication and enthusiasm for the cause. After his first edit-a-thon, he began participating in global LGBTQIA+ Wiki meetings and groups. In 2025, he spoke at the Queering Wikipedia online conference. In Nigeria, he organizes virtual training on Wikidata for young queer editors and has led campaigns such as the Trans Visibility Challenge (2025).
snippet: Last month, we had the opportunity to speak with Obinna Tony-Francis Ochem (Lagos, Nigeria, 1996, pronouns: he/they). Obinna, who lives in Lagos, is a Nigerian writer, human rights researcher,
creator: Mwvh

title: Achievement unlocked! The Wikimedia Bolivia-Japan Friendship made history
content:
During May and June, the Bolivian community got to work improving content on bilateral relations between Bolivia and Japan and enriching content related to Japanese culture in Bolivia. These activities were part of the Wikimedia Bolivia-Japan Friendship, a campaign coordinated between the user groups of both countries to strengthen community ties and improve content about both nations on Wikimedia projects. To learn more about the origins of this friendship, we invite you to read this post and this post.

During the whole month of May, volunteer photographers from the Bolivian community visited several dojos in La Paz to capture the growing interest that Japanese martial arts have been generating in Bolivia for decades. They also photographed bonsai trees and cosplay events, where they caught the excitement and passion of the cosplayers. Bolivia, after all, is a country that truly loves Japanese culture.
snippet: During May and June, the Bolivian community got to work improving content on bilateral relations between Bolivia and Japan and enriching content related to Japanese culture in Bolivia. These
creator: Carlillasa
